front: Poem: the fifth elegy addresses Picasso's Family of Saltimbanques, asking 'But who are they, tell me, these Travellers?'
Poem: the tenth elegy describes Lament leading a boy to the 'Mountains of Primal Grief' through a 'City of Grief' with constellations like 'the Burning Book'
Poem: in the fourth elegy, the speaker gazes at a puppet stage and declares 'finally, a real play!' when an angel brings puppets to life; draws on Kleist's 'On the Marionette Theatre'
Poem: references an urn inscribed 'Subrisio Saltat' and invokes the 'days of Tobias'
Poem: the torso 'glistens like a wild beast's fur' and is 'suffused with brilliance inside'; has a 'dark center where procreation flared'
Poem: Ocean Vuong responded with 'Torso of Air' ('Suppose you do change your life'); Mary Oliver's 'The Swan' was also inspired by the closing line
Poem: opens 'There rose a Tree. O pure transcendence!'; instructs the reader to 'erect no gravestone' for the title figure
Poem: 'Spring has come again. The earth is like a child that knows poems by heart'
Poem: a caged creature paces 'in cramped circles' in a 'ritual dance around a center / in which a mighty will stands paralyzed'
Poem: 'the curtain of the pupil soundlessly parts' and 'an image enters in' before it 'plunges into the heart and is gone'
Work: advises a military cadet 'ask yourself in the most silent hour of your night: must I write?'; praises J.P. Jacobsen and the Bible as the two 'indispensable' books
back: Letters to a Young Poet (Rainer Maria Rilke)

front: Work: compares appreciating loneliness to puberty; states 'What we need, after all, is only: solitude'
back: Letters to a Young Poet (Rainer Maria Rilke)

front: Novel: the narrator recounts how an iron amulet became embedded in the rotting chest of the mad king Charles VI
back: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (Rainer Maria Rilke)
work: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge

front: Novel: tells the story of the False Dmitri, happy as an impostor until believed to be real; ends with a prodigal son interpretation
back: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (Rainer Maria Rilke)
work: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge

front: Novel: the title character watches doctors pierce his father's heart per a provision in the will; recalls his uncle dying of dropsy carried by servants
back: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge (Rainer Maria Rilke)
work: The Notebooks of Malte Laurids Brigge

front: Poem: a woman 'fans her dress into passionate flames'; 'naked arms' like 'rattlesnakes'; likened to a 'sulphur match'
Poem: 'Lord it is time. Great was the summer feast'; 'He who has not built his house, will not start now'
Poem: Eurydice is 'already loosened like long hair' and 'already root,' does not recognize her lover
Poem: includes The Book of Monastic Life and The Book of Poverty and Death; dedicated to Lou Andreas-Salome; drawn from Orthodox Christianity in Russia
Poem: 'And now and then there's a white elephant' among animals in a 'blind and breathless game'
Poem: dedicated to Paula Modersohn-Becker; 'practice on this: letting each other go. For holding on comes easily'
Poet: W.H. Auden called this author the 'Santa Claus of loneliness' in response to his 'thing-poems'
Poet: John Berryman flatly asserted this author 'was a jerk' in his Dream Songs
Poet: Ludwig Wittgenstein gave this author 20,000 crowns via magazine editor Ludwig von Ficker
Poet: traded letters with Marina Tsvetaeva and Boris Pasternak in the summer of 1926
